title: Cooking Oil / Grease on Cotton
stainChemistry: Oil and grease stains (cooking oil, motor oil, cosmetics, sunscreen) contain lipid molecules that repel water. Fresh stains sit on the fiber surface; aged stains penetrate and can degrade the fiber's polymers. POG (POG remover) is a volatile dry solvent with strong affinity for lipids — it dissolves the oil droplets and lifts them away. VDS (Volatile Dry Solvent) provides a gentler alternative. Steam and heat activate the solvent's vapor pressure, improving penetration. Post-treatment with NSD removes residual solvent and oil micro-droplets. Cotton (cellulose) can absorb oils deeply into the fiber structure.
whyThisWorks: Oil molecules are non-polar — they don't mix with water. Regular washing pushes oil around rather than removing it. POG is a volatile dry solvent with high lipid affinity — it dissolves oil droplets on contact and can be blotted away. Amyl acetate handles older or polymerized oil. NSD emulsifies any remaining micro-droplets so water can flush them. Water must NOT be applied before solvent — it causes the oil to emulsify and spread deeper into the fiber.
defaultAssumption: Treat as unknown age / possibly dried.

steps: 1. Apply POG (POG remover) directly to the oil stain. Tamp gently and blot with absorbent cloth. Dwell 5–10 minutes. POG is a volatile dry solvent with strong lipid affinity — it dissolves oil droplets on contact. For delicate fabrics, use VDS (Volatile Dry Solvent) instead.

2. If POG plateaus, apply amyl acetate (banana oil solvent) for stronger solvent action. Dwell 3–5 minutes. Especially effective on older or polymerized oil stains. Blot with clean absorbent cloth between applications. Never use on acetate fabrics.

3. Apply NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) to emulsify any remaining oil micro-droplets and remove solvent residue. Dwell 5 minutes. The detergent surrounds oil particles and allows them to be flushed away. Rinse thoroughly with cool water.
4. Apply 28% Acetic acid (diluted 1:10) to restore fiber to its natural state. Dwell 1–2 minutes. Blot dry with clean cloth and allow to air dry.
professionalProtocol.temperature: ambient — do NOT apply heat before solvent treatment. Heat polymerizes oil and makes removal much harder. Warm water (not hot) acceptable for the final NSD flush after solvent extraction.
warnings: Using water before solvent — water causes the oil to emulsify and spread into the fiber, making it harder to remove.
Over-saturation with solvent — excess solvent spreads the stain and leaves a residue ring.
Not using proper ventilation — solvents produce fumes that require extraction.
Applying heat without solvent — heat can polymerize the oil and make removal harder.
Never use amyl acetate on acetate fabrics.

steps: 1. Sprinkle baking soda or cornstarch on the stain. Wait 5 minutes. The powder soaks up surface oil before it sinks deeper into the fabric.
2. Brush off the powder with a clean white cloth. This removes the oil the powder absorbed.
3. Put a drop of undiluted Dawn-style dish soap on the stain. Blot it in. mild dish soap is made to cut through grease and oil.
4. Let it sit for 2–3 minutes. Rinse with cold water.
5. Repeat if needed. Air dry — don't use a dryer until the stain is gone.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: If the stain persists after NSD, try IPA (IPA) on a cotton swab and dab the stain. If it survives two rounds, take it to a professional cleaner.
warnings: Never add water to an oil stain first — water and oil don't mix, and you'll spread it.
Test any cleaning solution on a hidden seam or inside hem first.
Avoid heat until the stain is completely gone — heat polymerizes oil and sets the stain.
Always blot — never rub. Rubbing pushes the stain deeper.

neverDo: Never apply water before solvent — causes oil to emulsify and spread deeper into the fiber.
Never apply heat before solvent removal — polymerizes oil and makes removal much harder.
Never put in the dryer before stain is fully gone.
Never use amyl acetate on acetate or triacetate fabrics.
fiberSensitivities: Acetate and triacetate cannot tolerate solvents — use NSD only on these fibers.
Delicate wools should use VDS instead of harsh POG.

escalation.when: If VDS/POG reaches saturation, the oil may have polymerized or oxidized into the fiber polymer. Try sequential treating: first VDS to lift surface oil, then low-pH detergent to emulsify residual particles. For automotive greases (polyunsaturated), amyl acetate provides stronger solvent action than VDS.
escalation.whatToTell: We've treated the grease on your garment. Grease repels water, so regular washing pushes it around rather than removing it — that's why specialized solvent treatment is needed.

contextQuestions: How old is the stain — was it treated immediately or has it been sitting?
Was water applied to the stain before solvent treatment?
What type of grease is it — cooking oil, motor oil, cosmetic/sunscreen, or other?
Has the garment been through a dryer since the stain occurred?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Fresh cooking oil on cotton responds well to baking soda absorption + NSD. Aged or polymerized oil (especially motor oil or sunscreen) requires POG and potentially amyl acetate. Water applied before solvent has spread the stain and may require a larger treatment area. A garment run through the dryer with an oil stain is significantly harder to recover — the oil has thermally bonded to the fiber.
